title: Aurora Watch
desc: The mirror line has to be ready before aurora, starfall, and castle light begin writing themselves across the lake.

steps.text:
At dusk, Lake Ken-I-Po stops looking like a place and starts looking like a held breath. Najarin's castle rises from the shore in pale tiers above the water, every high window gathering the last gold of evening while the first blue of night settles over the lake. The surface is so smooth that the towers seem to continue downward into a second castle made of reflection. Out over the eastern dark, thin stars begin to fall one by one, short white strokes above the horizon. By full night the aurora will arrive and lay green, silver, and rose across all of it at once. The mirror line along the bank has to be ready before that happens.

A work slate weighted under a polished stone offers the two jobs that matter most: clear the shoreline mirrors for the night's reading, or reset the watch skiff so it can sit perfectly still among the colors.

options.label: Polish the shoreline mirrors for the night reading
Reset the watch skiff's mooring lines and fenders

steps.text:
The shoreline mirrors are shallow plates set low to the water so they can catch sky, lake, and castle in one measured angle. Damp has filmed them dull. You wipe each plate clear, true the frames, and tip the mounts a fraction until the etched guides hold exactly what they should: the dark line of shore, the pale face of Najarin's castle, and the first streaks of starfall above it. Then the aurora begins.

Color comes softly at first, a green wash under the constellations, then silver, then a faint rose that turns the water into shifting glass. Every cleaned mirror answers with its own smaller sky. By the time you cap the last plate, the bank looks less like equipment than a row of quiet windows opened onto the same impossible night.

options.label: Cap the last mirror and leave the shoreline glowing

steps.text:
The watch skiff is meant to sit just far enough from shore that castle light and shore shadow stop interfering with the readings. You loosen one line, draw another taut, replace a worn fender braid, and test the hull until it rides exactly where the lake wants it and no farther. When it settles, it barely disturbs the surface. The boat seems suspended between the castle above and the castle below, each tower doubled in black water.

By full dark, auroral color is moving across the lake in long translucent sheets. A falling star cuts through them and breaks apart in reflection before it reaches the far shore. The skiff holds steady at the center of it all, a single dark shape in green and silver fire. That is the trick at Ken-I-Po: the place invites wonder, then asks for precision.

options.label: Tie off the last line and leave the watch skiff ready
